verses.text: Then Solomon began to build the house of the LORD at Jerusalem in mount Moriah, where the LORD appeared unto David his father, in the place that David had prepared in the threshingfloor of Ornan the Jebusite. And he began to build in the second day of the second month, in the fourth year of his reign. Guidance: At last the building begins, and the Chronicler is careful to tell us exactly where: 'in mount Moriah, where the LORD appeared unto David his father, in the place that David had prepared in the threshingfloor of Ornan the Jebusite.' This is holy ground with a long history. It was here that the LORD appeared to David and stayed the destroying angel when David offered sacrifice (1 Chron 21); and Mount Moriah was the very place where, centuries before, Abraham had offered up Isaac and God provided a substitute ram (Gen 22). The chapter is showing us that the temple rose on the place of atonement — the spot where judgment was met by mercy through sacrifice, where God appeared and answered by fire, where Abraham's son was spared by a provided offering. The chapter is teaching us that the worship of God is founded on sacrifice and atonement; the house was built precisely where blood had been shed and mercy shown, so that its very location preached the gospel of substitution. The chapter is teaching us the wonderful continuity of God's redemptive purpose across the centuries: Moriah, the mount of Abraham's offering, became the mount of David's altar, and now the mount of the temple — one place, hallowed again and again by sacrifice, pointing forward to the ultimate sacrifice that would be offered near this same Jerusalem, when God's own Son, the true Isaac, would not be spared but given for us all. The chapter is teaching us that God remembers the places of His grace and builds upon them; the threshingfloor where the sword was sheathed became the dwelling place of His name. The chapter is teaching us to trace, with wonder, the thread of sacrifice running from Abraham to David to Solomon's temple and on to the cross — for the LORD's house is always built on the ground of atonement, and all true worship rests on the sacrifice God Himself provides.

And he began to build in the second day of the second month, in the fourth year of his reign. The chapter is teaching us that the worship of God is founded on sacrifice and atonement; the house was built precisely where blood had been shed and mercy shown, so that its very location preached the gospel of substitution. The chapter is teaching us the wonderful continuity of God's redemptive purpose across the centuries: Moriah, the mount of Abraham's offering, became the mount of David's altar, and now the mount of the temple — one place, hallowed again and again by sacrifice, pointing forward to the ultimate sacrifice that would be offered near this same Jerusalem, when God's own Son, the true Isaac, would not be spared but given for us all. The chapter is teaching us to trace, with wonder, the thread of sacrifice running from Abraham to David to Solomon's temple and on to the cross — for the LORD's house is always built on the ground of atonement, and all true worship rests on the sacrifice God Himself provides.

Now these are the things wherein Solomon was instructed for the building of the house of God. The length by cubits after the first measure was threescore cubits, and the breadth twenty cubits. And the porch that was in the front of the house, the length of it was according to the breadth of the house, twenty cubits, and the height was an hundred and twenty: and he overlaid it within with pure gold. And the greater house he cieled with fir tree, which he overlaid with fine gold, and set thereon palm trees and chains. And he garnished the house with precious stones for beauty: and the gold was gold of Parvaim. He overlaid also the house, the beams, the posts, and the walls thereof, and the doors thereof, with gold; and graved cherubims on the walls. And he made the most holy house, the length whereof was according to the breadth of the house, twenty cubits, and the breadth thereof twenty cubits: and he overlaid it with fine gold, amounting to six hundred talents. And the weight of the nails was fifty shekels of gold. And he overlaid the upper chambers with gold. Guidance: The Chronicler describes the lavish beauty of the temple: overlaid throughout with fine gold, garnished with precious stones, adorned with carved palm trees, chains, and cherubim — even the nails were of gold. The most holy place alone was overlaid with six hundred talents of fine gold. No expense was spared; everything was made as glorious as human skill and wealth could make it. The chapter is showing us the surpassing richness and beauty of the house built for God; it gleamed with gold from floor to ceiling, a fitting reflection of the glory of the One worshiped there. The chapter is teaching us that the worship of God is worthy of beauty and our best; Solomon did not build a plain or grudging house, but lavished gold and precious stones upon it, for nothing was too good for the LORD. We are taught that there is a place for beauty, richness, and care in the things of God — not for ostentation, but to honour Him and to lift the heart toward His glory. The chapter is teaching us that the gold-covered house, dazzling in its splendour, was meant to declare the worth and majesty of God to all who entered; the very walls preached His glory. The chapter is teaching us, in the carved cherubim and palm trees, that the temple was made not only rich but beautiful, adorned with images of the heavenly and the flourishing — a place that spoke of paradise and the presence of God. And yet we are taught to remember, with Solomon's own words from the previous chapter, that the heaven of heavens cannot contain God; all this gold was but a faint shadow of His true glory. The chapter is teaching us to honour God with beauty and our best, while never imagining that any earthly splendour could match His worth — for the most golden house on earth is but a dim reflection of the glory of Him who dwells in light unapproachable, and whose true temple is now His people, adorned not with gold but with the beauty of holiness.

And in the most holy house he made two cherubims of image work, and overlaid them with gold. And the wings of the cherubims were twenty cubits long: one wing of the one cherub was five cubits, reaching to the wall of the house: and the other wing was likewise five cubits, reaching to the wing of the other cherub. And one wing of the other cherub was five cubits, reaching to the wall of the house: and the other wing was five cubits also, joining to the wing of the other cherub. The wings of these cherubims spread themselves forth twenty cubits: and they stood on their feet, and their faces were inward. Guidance: In the most holy place Solomon set two great cherubim of gold, their outstretched wings spanning the whole width of the room — twenty cubits — overshadowing the place where the ark would rest, their faces turned inward. The chapter is showing us the cherubim, those angelic figures that guard and attend the presence of God, standing in the innermost sanctuary above the mercy seat. The chapter is teaching us the awesome holiness of God's dwelling place; the most holy place was no ordinary room, but the throne-room of the LORD, attended by the golden cherubim with wings outspread, where God was enthroned 'between the cherubims' (Ps 80:1). The chapter is teaching us that the presence of God is holy and to be approached with reverence; the cherubim, like the flaming sword at Eden's gate, mark the sacredness of the divine presence and the awe with which it must be regarded. The chapter is teaching us, in the wings spread protectively over the place of the ark and the mercy seat, the truth of God's gracious presence enthroned above the place of atonement — for it was upon the mercy seat, between the cherubim, that the blood of atonement was sprinkled, and there God met with His people. The chapter is teaching us that heaven itself, with its angelic hosts, attends the worship and presence of God; the cherubim in the temple were earthly images of the heavenly reality, where angels forever surround the throne and worship Him who sits upon it. The chapter is teaching us to draw near to God with reverence and holy fear, mindful that His presence is attended by glory and guarded by His holiness — and to rejoice that, through the atonement, sinful people are granted access to the very place the cherubim overshadow, made welcome at the mercy seat through the blood of Christ, our great atonement and our peace.

The chapter is teaching us, in the wings spread protectively over the place of the ark and the mercy seat, the truth of God's gracious presence enthroned above the place of atonement — for it was upon the mercy seat, between the cherubim, that the blood of atonement was sprinkled, and there God met with His people. The chapter is teaching us that heaven itself, with its angelic hosts, attends the worship and presence of God; the cherubim in the temple were earthly images of the heavenly reality, where angels forever surround the throne and worship Him who sits upon it. The chapter is teaching us to draw near to God with reverence and holy fear, mindful that His presence is attended by glory and guarded by His holiness — and to rejoice that, through the atonement, sinful people are granted access to the very place the cherubim overshadow, made welcome at the mercy seat through the blood of Christ, our great atonement and our peace.

And he made the vail of blue, and purple, and crimson, and fine linen, and wrought cherubims thereon. Also he made before the house two pillars of thirty and five cubits high, and the chapiter that was on the top of each of them was five cubits. And he made chains, as in the oracle, and put them on the heads of the pillars; and made an hundred pomegranates, and put them on the chains. And he reared up the pillars before the temple, one on the right hand, and the other on the left; and called the name of that on the right hand Jachin, and the name of that on the left Boaz. Guidance: Two final features close the description: the veil of blue, purple, crimson, and fine linen, embroidered with cherubim, which screened the most holy place; and the two great bronze pillars set before the temple, named Jachin and Boaz. The veil guarded the holiest place, barring the way into God's immediate presence — a barrier that proclaimed, until Christ came, that 'the way into the holiest of all was not yet made manifest' (Heb 9:8). The chapter is showing us that the holiest place was veiled, set apart, not freely open — a solemn witness to the holiness of God and the distance sin had put between Him and us. The chapter is teaching us the meaning of that veil, which would one day be torn from top to bottom at the death of Christ (Matt 27:51), declaring that the way to God is now opened through His blood; what the temple veil shut, the cross flung wide. The chapter is teaching us, in the two pillars, a standing sermon in their very names: Jachin means 'He shall establish,' and Boaz means 'In Him is strength.' Set at the entrance of the house, they testified to every worshiper who passed between them that God establishes His house, His people, and His kingdom, and that in Him alone is their strength. The chapter is teaching us to read these pillars as a confession of faith: our standing is established by God, not by ourselves, and our strength is in Him. Whatever endures, endures because He establishes it; whatever stands strong, stands by His might. The chapter is teaching us to enter God's presence resting on these two truths — that He will establish us and that in Him is our strength — and to rejoice that the veil which once barred the way has been torn by Christ, so that we who were once shut out may now enter the holiest with boldness, established and strengthened by the God who has opened the way. Jachin and Boaz stand at the door still, preaching to all who would worship: He shall establish; in Him is strength.
